ollama:
OLLAMA LOCAL API (offline, 127.0.0.1, default port 11434). All endpoints are POST with a JSON body; localhost binding is the default (set OLLAMA_HOST=127.0.0.1:11434 explicitly to be sure it never binds 0.0.0.0). No auth header is required for a loopback server.

PRIMARY ENDPOINT — POST http://127.0.0.1:11434/api/chat
Request body fields:
  - model        (string, REQUIRED) e.g. "llama3.1:8b-instruct-q8_0" — must be a tag already pulled (`ollama pull`). Pin the exact tag+quant, not "latest".
  - messages     (array, REQUIRED) [{"role":"system|user|assistant","content":"..."}]. role "tool" is also accepted for tool results.
  - stream       (REQUIRED set to false) — without it Ollama streams NDJSON (one JSON object per line) and your single-shot parser breaks.
  - format       (object | "json") — pass a FULL JSON Schema object here for structured output (see structured_output). The literal string "json" only guarantees syntactic JSON, not your schema — prefer the schema object.
  - options      (object) decoding knobs: {"temperature":0,"seed":42,"num_predict":512,"num_ctx":8192,"top_k":1,"top_p":1,"repeat_penalty":1.0}. These map to llama.cpp sampler params. temperature 0 + a fixed seed is what makes it reproducible.
  - keep_alive   (string, e.g. "30m" or "-1" to pin in RAM) — avoids reload latency between scheduled calls.
RESPONSE (stream:false) is a SINGLE JSON object. The text is at message.content:  resp$message$content (R) / _ollj.message.content via the JSON libname (SAS). Also returns done:true, done_reason, total_duration, prompt_eval_count, eval_count (token accounting), and (when format=schema) message.content is a JSON STRING you parse a second time.

ALTERNATE — POST /api/generate : single-prompt (no roles). Body {"model","prompt","system","stream":false,"format":<schema>,"options":{...},"raw":false}. Text comes back at .response (not .message.content). Use /api/chat for system+user separation; /api/generate when you have one flat prompt.

UTILITY (GET, no body): /api/tags (list pulled models — use to assert the model exists before calling), /api/version (liveness probe), /api/ps (loaded models). POST /api/show {"model":...} returns the modelfile, template, parameters and the model's digest/sha256 — capture that digest for repro provenance. POST /api/embeddings {"model","prompt"} -> .embedding for RAG.
OpenAI-COMPAT shim also exists at /v1/chat/completions (see llamacpp field) if you want one code path across both servers.
llamacpp:
LLAMA.CPP SERVER (the `llama-server` binary from llama.cpp; offline, 127.0.0.1, default port 8080). Started e.g.:
  llama-server -m /models/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q8_0.gguf --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080 --ctx-size 8192 --seed 42 --no-webui
Two API surfaces:

1) NATIVE — POST http://127.0.0.1:8080/completion  (note: singular, no /v1). Body:
   {"prompt":"<full templated prompt>","n_predict":512,"temperature":0,"seed":42,"top_k":1,"top_p":1.0,"min_p":0,"repeat_penalty":1.0,"cache_prompt":true,
    "json_schema":{...},                <-- structured output the easy way (server compiles schema->GBNF)
    "grammar":"root ::= ...",            <-- OR raw GBNF directly (mutually exclusive with json_schema)
    "stream":false}
   Response: a single JSON object; the text is at .content. Token info in .tokens_predicted / .tokens_evaluated; .stop true. The native endpoint wants an already-templated prompt (apply the model's chat template yourself), or use /v1 below which applies it for you.

2) OpenAI-COMPATIBLE — POST http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions (this is the recommended path; it applies the GGUF's built-in chat template). Body:
   {"model":"local","messages":[{"role":"system",...},{"role":"user",...}],
    "temperature":0,"seed":42,"max_tokens":512,"top_p":1,
    "response_format":{"type":"json_schema","json_schema":{"name":"r","strict":true,"schema":{...}}}}
   Modern llama-server supports response_format with BOTH "json_object" and "json_schema" (strict). Internally it converts the schema to GBNF and constrains sampling, so the schema is ENFORCED, not merely requested.
   Response is OpenAI-shaped: choices[0].message.content holds the JSON string; usage{} has token counts. GET /health -> {"status":"ok"} for the liveness probe; GET /v1/models lists the loaded model; GET /props returns the model path + default generation settings.

ONE GATEWAY, TWO BACKENDS: point your macro at /v1/chat/completions on EITHER server (Ollama also exposes it on 11434) and the request body is identical except response_format vs format. That lets one SAS macro / one R function target both by swapping host:port + the schema-field name.
decoding:
REPRODUCIBILITY = make the sampler deterministic AND pin the exact model artifact. Greedy decoding alone is necessary but NOT sufficient.

Sampler (set ALL of these):
  - temperature = 0  -> Ollama/llama.cpp both collapse to greedy argmax (no probabilistic sampling). This is the dominant lever.
  - seed = <fixed int> (e.g. 42) -> deterministic tie-breaking / any residual stochastic path. Ollama: options.seed; llama.cpp: "seed" in body or --seed at launch. With temp 0 the seed rarely matters but set it anyway so a later non-zero temp is still reproducible.
  - top_k = 1, top_p = 1.0, min_p = 0, repeat_penalty = 1.0 -> neutralize the rest of the sampler chain so nothing reorders the argmax.
  - num_predict / n_predict / max_tokens = a fixed cap -> identical truncation every run.
  - num_ctx / --ctx-size fixed -> a different context window changes rope/attention and can change tokens; pin it.

Artifact (the part people forget):
  - Pin model + QUANTIZATION + DIGEST. "llama3.1:8b" is not enough — use the explicit quant tag (…-q8_0) and record the sha256 from Ollama /api/show or llama.cpp /props. A re-pull or a requant changes weights -> changes output. Store {model_tag, quant, sha256, server_version} alongside every result for an auditable, frozen-model run (your local-LLM "frozen model = frozen behavior" repro argument).
  - Same backend build: a llama.cpp version bump can change kernels/rounding. Capture /api/version or the llama-server build string.

CAVEAT (be honest with the reviewer): even greedy decoding is only reproducible on the SAME hardware/build. GPU non-associative FP reductions, batch/parallel-slot scheduling, and flash-attention kernels can produce rare last-token divergence across machines. For strict determinism keep one validated box, num_parallel=1 / one slot, and treat cross-GPU bit-identity as not guaranteed — pin the box, not just the params.
structured_output:
MANDATORY — never trust a free-text LLM into a clinical pipeline. Constrain the GRAMMAR so only schema-valid tokens can be sampled; then the downstream SAS/R parse is total, not best-effort.

OLLAMA: pass a full JSON Schema OBJECT as the top-level "format" field (not inside options):
  "format": {"type":"object",
             "properties":{"category":{"type":"string","enum":["ON TRACK","AT RISK","DELAYED"]},
                           "confidence":{"type":"number"},
                           "rationale":{"type":"string"}},
             "required":["category","confidence","rationale"],
             "additionalProperties": false}
Ollama compiles this to a GBNF grammar and constrains decoding, so message.content is GUARANTEED parseable JSON matching the shape. Set additionalProperties:false and use enum for every controlled field so the model literally cannot emit an off-list token. (Tip: still keep temperature 0; constrained + sampled can wander within the grammar.)

LLAMA.CPP: two equivalent routes —
  /completion  : "json_schema": { ...same schema... }  (server compiles to GBNF), or hand-write "grammar":"root ::= \"{\" ... " in GBNF directly for total control.
  /v1/chat/completions : "response_format":{"type":"json_schema","json_schema":{"name":"qc","strict":true,"schema":{...}}}  — strict:true enforces it. "json_object" is the weaker syntactic-only mode.

WHY MANDATORY: with grammar constraint the enum fields can ONLY be allowlist values, so the model output is pre-narrowed to your controlled vocabulary at SAMPLE time; the SAS/R validator (next field) is then defense-in-depth, not the only line of defense. Without it the model can wrap JSON in prose ("Here is the result: {…}"), emit markdown fences, hallucinate a 4th category, or return invalid JSON — all of which silently corrupt a tracker. Schema-constrain at the server AND allowlist-validate at the client: belt and suspenders, exactly as required for a regulated deliverable.
no_egress:
ASSERT LOOPBACK + OFFLINE BEFORE EVERY RUN (encode it, don't assume it):
1) URL allowlist in code: the base URL macro/var is hard-asserted to start with http://127.0.0.1: or http://localhost: (or [::1]). %ss-style %assert FAILS LOUD and aborts if the host is anything else — no macro is allowed to call a non-loopback URL. Reject any model_url containing a routable host.
2) Server binding: launch the backend bound to loopback only — Ollama: OLLAMA_HOST=127.0.0.1:11434 (and do NOT set 0.0.0.0); llama.cpp: --host 127.0.0.1. So even a misconfigured client can't reach it from off-box.
3) Liveness probe, not network call: before work, GET /api/version (Ollama) or /health (llama.cpp) on 127.0.0.1 with a short timeout; a non-200 aborts. This proves the LOCAL server is up without touching any external host.
4) PROC HTTP / httr2 must NOT carry a proxy: in SAS clear PROXYHOST/PROXYPORT (or never set them) and rely on the literal 127.0.0.1 URL; in R set req_proxy() unused and optionally httr2::req_options(noproxy="127.0.0.1,localhost"). Loopback never transits a proxy anyway, but assert no proxy env (http_proxy/https_proxy) is applied to the call.
5) Provenance line in every output JSON/log: {endpoint:"http://127.0.0.1:11434", offline:true} so an auditor sees the call never left the box. Optionally, at the OS layer, the validated box runs the model service with egress firewalled; the macro just refuses to believe it's offline unless the host is loopback. The SAS/R guard (loopback-host assertion) is the in-code "no PHI leaves the building" boundary — analogous to the SHEETLINK ops-only allowlist, but here it guards the network destination.
validator:
VALIDATOR PATTERN — parse, then check EVERY field against an explicit allowlist/enum/range; reject+log on any miss; NEVER let free text flow downstream. Grammar-constraint at the server narrows the output; the validator is the authoritative gate.

Steps (identical in SAS and R):
1) Parse the model's content string as JSON (SAS: write message.content to a fileref, LIBNAME JSON it; R: jsonlite::fromJSON / httr2::resp_body_json). A parse failure = HARD REJECT (this catches prose/markdown-fence leakage if grammar were ever bypassed).
2) Field presence: assert every REQUIRED key exists and is non-missing. Missing key = reject.
3) Allowlist / enum: each controlled field (e.g. category) must be %sysfunc(indexw(...)) / %in% a hard-coded VALID set, UPCASE-normalized — exactly the SHEETLINK %ss_guard idea applied to model OUTPUT. An off-list value (a hallucinated 4th category) = reject, even though the grammar should have prevented it (defense in depth).
4) Type/range: numeric fields cast and bounds-checked (0<=confidence<=1); non-numeric or out-of-range = reject.
5) On any miss: do NOT write to the tracker. Log ERROR with the offending field+value, emit the fail-loud email/return code (SAS &rc=1 + %abort path; R stop()), and quarantine the raw response for review. Fail LOUD, never silently coerce.
6) Provenance: stamp accepted records with {model_tag, sha256, seed, temperature, server_version, endpoint} so each value is traceable to a frozen model. Only records passing ALL checks are eligible to be written (and even then, a human gate for anything consequential).
Principle: the LLM is an UNTRUSTED text source; the allowlist validator is the trust boundary. Treat a non-conforming response as a failure, not as data.
r_snippet:
## llm_chat(): local Ollama, schema-constrained, temp0+seed, httr2+jsonlite, then allowlist-validate
suppressPackageStartupMessages({library(httr2); library(jsonlite)})
LLM_BASE <- "http://127.0.0.1:11434"
VALID_CATEGORY <- c("ON TRACK","AT RISK","DELAYED")

llm_chat <- function(model, sys, usr, base = LLM_BASE) {
  stopifnot(grepl("^http://(127\\.0\\.0\\.1|localhost)\\b", base))   # GUARD: loopback only, no egress

  schema <- list(type="object",
    properties=list(category=list(type="string", enum=VALID_CATEGORY),
                    confidence=list(type="number"), rationale=list(type="string")),
    required=list("category","confidence","rationale"), additionalProperties=FALSE)

  resp <- request(base) |>
    req_url_path_append("api","chat") |>
    req_body_json(list(model=model, stream=FALSE,
      options=list(temperature=0, seed=42L, top_k=1L, top_p=1, num_predict=512L),
      format=schema,                                   # server-side grammar constraint
      messages=list(list(role="system",content=sys),
                    list(role="user",  content=usr))), auto_unbox=TRUE) |>
    req_timeout(120) |>
    req_perform() |>
    resp_body_json()

  out <- fromJSON(resp$message$content)                # message.content is a JSON string -> parse again

  ## VALIDATOR: enum allowlist + range; any miss -> stop() (fail loud, no tracker write)
  out$category <- toupper(out$category)
  if (!out$category %in% VALID_CATEGORY)
    stop(sprintf("[LLM] off-allowlist category: %s", out$category), call.=FALSE)
  if (!is.numeric(out$confidence) || out$confidence < 0 || out$confidence > 1)
    stop(sprintf("[LLM] confidence out of range: %s", out$confidence), call.=FALSE)

  c(out, list(model=model, endpoint=base, seed=42L, temperature=0))   # provenance stamp
}
## llm_chat("llama3.1:8b-instruct-q8_0", "You classify deliverable status.", "ADPC due 15JUN, 60% done, 1 query open.")
pitfalls:
REAL GOTCHAS (early 2026):
1) STREAMING ON BY DEFAULT. Ollama /api/chat and /api/generate stream NDJSON unless you send "stream":false; PROC HTTP / a single resp_body_json then sees many objects and fails or grabs only the first chunk. Always set stream:false (llama.cpp native /completion also streams if "stream":true — keep it false).
2) MODEL MUST BE PULLED FIRST. `ollama pull llama3.1:8b-instruct-q8_0` before any call; an unknown tag returns 404 "model not found". Assert against GET /api/tags at startup. llama.cpp: the GGUF must be the one passed to -m at launch; you can't switch models per-request without a server that loaded it.
3) JSON SOMETIMES WRAPPED IN PROSE if NOT grammar-constrained. With only a prompt ("reply in JSON") or format:"json", models still emit ```json fences, a preamble, or a hallucinated extra field. ALWAYS use the full JSON-Schema (format=schema / json_schema / GBNF) so it's constrained at sample time — then validate anyway.
4) DOUBLE-PARSE. The schema result comes back as a JSON STRING inside message.content (Ollama) / choices[0].message.content (compat) — you parse the envelope, then parse content again. Forgetting the second parse leaves you holding a string, not fields.
5) CONTEXT LIMIT silently truncates. num_ctx (Ollama, default often 4096–8192) / --ctx-size (llama.cpp) caps prompt+output; a long QC dump overflows and the model answers about a truncated input. Set num_ctx explicitly, count prompt_eval_count vs the limit, and chunk. num_predict too small truncates the JSON mid-object -> parse fail (cap generously or the closing brace gets cut).
6) QUANTIZATION AFFECTS ADHERENCE & VALUES. A heavy quant (q4) is more likely to drift off the grammar's intent, miscount, or pick a weaker category than q8/fp16. Quant is part of the artifact identity — a requant changes outputs, so pin the exact quant tag AND its sha256; don't compare q4 results to a q8 baseline.
7) DETERMINISM IS PER-BOX. temp 0 + seed gives run-to-run determinism on one machine/build, but GPU FP non-associativity, parallel slots (set Ollama OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=1 / llama.cpp one slot), flash-attention, and a backend version bump can shift a token. Pin hardware + server build; don't promise cross-GPU bit-identity.
8) keep_alive / cold load. First call after idle reloads the model (seconds–minutes) and can time out a short PROC HTTP timeout; set keep_alive:"-1" (or a long value) for scheduled jobs and give httr2/PROC HTTP a generous timeout.
9) Loopback ≠ authenticated. A localhost Ollama has NO auth; anything on the box can call it. Bind 127.0.0.1 only and rely on OS isolation — and never send PHI you wouldn't want another local process to read, even though it never leaves the machine.
10) NUMBER FORMATTING. JSON numbers must be bare (0.85 not "0.85"); when SAS builds bodies by hand, don't quote numeric option values, and when reading back, a number the model emits as a string fails range checks — coerce and validate type.
